skill.name: route-tester
skill.description: Test authenticated API routes with cookie-based JWT authentication, local route scripts, curl examples, and mock authentication patterns for development environments.
skill.summary: Guide AI agents through authenticated local API route testing and response verification.
skill.author: diet103

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and sensitive configuration indicators in SKILL.md. Most backtick and localhost URL hits are Markdown examples, but the audit confirms hardcoded credentials, mock authentication bypass guidance, and sensitive config file handling. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, but this community skill should not be published without sanitization.

high_findings.title: Hardcoded Credentials and Auth Bypass Guidance
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documents test credentials, a MySQL root password in a command, and mock authentication variables that grant admin-like roles. These may be local examples, but marketplace users could reproduce unsafe credentials or authorization bypass patterns.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly lists usernames, passwords, mock admin roles, and a root database password. The context is testing, but the credentials and bypass settings are concrete and actionable.

medium_findings.title: Executable Shell Command Testing Instructions
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with limited scope. The static Ruby backtick findings are mostly Markdown code fences, but the skill intentionally instructs agents to run node, curl, docker, mysql, and pm2 commands. These commands are legitimate for local route testing, yet they can mutate services and databases.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are examples in Markdown, not executable source code. However, the skill purpose is to guide an agent to run them, including write-path API calls and database queries.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Environment and Config File Handling
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill tells users to rely on config.ini and .env values for JWT secrets and mock authentication. No exfiltration is present, but the instructions direct attention to files that commonly contain secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced files and settings are explicitly named and include JWT secret and mock auth configuration. The evidence does not show network exfiltration, so the severity remains below critical.

low_findings.title: Localhost Network URL Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for exfiltration. The hardcoded URLs are localhost service routes used for local API testing. They do not point to external collection endpoints, but they still require caution because they may trigger state-changing requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs all resolve to localhost ports in testing examples. I found no evidence of outbound exfiltration to a third-party domain.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The scanner flagged weak cryptography, but the cited lines only describe JWT route testing or unrelated example text. No weak algorithm implementation appears in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains documentation, not cryptographic code. The line 319 hit is a SQL ordering example and does not indicate DES or another weak algorithm.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Pattern False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines are SQL select examples and numbered test scenario comments. They do not enumerate host details or gather system inventory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to local database verification and comments in test examples. I found no commands that collect host reconnaissance data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mock Admin Authentication Enablement
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill describes enabling mock authentication and assigning admin or operations roles through headers and environment variables. This is useful in development but dangerous if copied into shared or production-like environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The guidance explicitly enables mock auth and privileged roles. The file states development-only limits, but marketplace reuse can lose that context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database Command Includes Root Password
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a Docker MySQL command with a root password and database name. This exposes a concrete credential pattern and encourages direct database access during route tests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command contains a root password in the command line. The second location repeats the same database access pattern for post-route verification.

content.value_statement: Authenticated API routes are hard to test when cookies, JWTs, and route prefixes interact. This skill gives structured local testing patterns for route calls, mock auth, and response checks.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to test authenticated local API endpoints with cookie-based JWT authentication.
Shows patterns for GET, POST, file upload, and workflow route checks.
Documents mock authentication setup for development and test environments.
Helps map service ports, route prefixes, and full endpoint URLs.
Provides debugging steps for 401, 403, 404, and 500 route failures.
Suggests database verification after state-changing route tests.
limitations: The skill is tailored to one project structure and service layout.
It assumes local services, Keycloak, and MySQL are available.
It includes unsafe example credentials that require sanitization before publishing.
It does not provide executable scripts inside the skill package.

use_cases.title: Validate a New API Route
use_cases.description: Build the full local endpoint URL, send an authenticated request, and confirm the expected response.

use_cases.title: Debug Authorization Failures
use_cases.description: Check token format, role requirements, route prefixes, and service health when a protected endpoint fails.

use_cases.title: Verify Workflow Side Effects
use_cases.description: Run route tests and inspect related database records after workflow submissions or step completions.

prompt_templates.title: Test One Route
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the route-tester skill to test this local authenticated endpoint: [METHOD] [URL]. Include the expected status and response fields.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Auth Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the route-tester skill to diagnose why this protected route returns [STATUS]. Check cookie auth, route prefix, roles, and service health.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Route Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the route-tester skill to test this changed route with valid and invalid inputs. Summarize response behavior and database side effects.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Authenticated Route Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the route-tester skill to design a local test plan for these authenticated routes. Include auth method, inputs, expected outputs, and verification steps.

output_examples.input: Test a protected workflow start route after a backend change.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the service port, constructs the full local route, runs an authenticated request, and reports status, response fields, and follow-up checks.

output_examples.input: Investigate a 403 response from a protected endpoint.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks role requirements, mock auth settings, cookie handling, route registration, and likely permission mismatches.

output_examples.input: Confirm that a form submission route writes expected database records.
output_examples.output: The assistant tests the route, reviews the response, and describes which database records should be inspected for verification.

best_practices: Use sanitized test credentials and project-specific secrets managed outside prompts.
Run state-changing route tests only against local or disposable environments.
Confirm route prefixes and service ports before sending authenticated requests.
anti_patterns: Do not copy mock authentication settings into production configuration.
Do not expose JWT secrets, database passwords, or bearer-equivalent cookies in logs.
Do not run database verification commands against shared data without approval.

faq.question: Does this skill include runnable code?
faq.answer: No. It provides route testing instructions and command patterns for an existing project.

faq.question: Which authentication style does it cover?
faq.answer: It focuses on cookie-based JWT authentication and optional mock authentication for development.

faq.question: Can it test production endpoints?
faq.answer: It should not be used against production endpoints. The examples are intended for local testing only.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: The skill includes hardcoded credentials, mock auth guidance, and direct database access examples.

faq.question: Does it send data to external servers?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The documented URLs point to localhost services.

faq.question: What should be changed before publishing?
faq.answer: Remove real-looking credentials, redact secret paths, and add strong warnings around mock authentication.
